query: What is the difference between pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements in CSS?

competitors.label: Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements
competitors.selectionReason: This MDN learning guide is the closest canonical documentation page for the exact compare question because it explains pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements together, not as isolated references.
theirStrengths: MDN directly explains the conceptual difference between state-based pseudo-classes and virtual-part pseudo-elements with official examples.
theirGaps: The guide is less interview-shaped and less compact about how to remember the distinction under pressure or how combined selectors like a:hover::after fit into one mental model.

realWorldUseCases:
Overview

Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements allow developers to target special states or parts of elements beyond basic selectors. A pseudo-class represents a state (like hover or focus), while a pseudo-element targets a portion of content (like the first line or a generated content block).
followUpConfusion:
Notable Differences
  • Pseudo-classes use a single colon (:), e.g. :hover.
  • Pseudo-elements use a double colon (::) to distinguish structural parts, e.g. ::before.
  • They can be combined — for instance: a:hover::after adds content only when hovered.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the two selector families accurately, but our page is more explicit about why the distinction matters in real UI work: interaction state versus generated or partial content.
actionableExamples: Its examples for :hover, :focus, ::before, and ::first-line are strong, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is more direct on the common follow-up confusion: single colon means state selection, double colon means a virtual part or generated piece, and they can be combined in one selector.

actionableExamples: Keep the combined-selector example visible so the page stays faster to scan than a broader learning guide.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical explainer, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it compresses the distinction into one practical rule: pseudo-classes answer when state changes, pseudo-elements answer which part or generated piece is being styled.

followUpConfusion:
Still so complicated?

Think of pseudo-classes as describing when an element behaves differently, and pseudo-elements as describing which part of it you’re styling or creating.
